title: Why Compress Context
concept: Reducing token count to fit more relevant information, cut cost, and reduce context-rot exposure.

exercises.prompt: Quick warm-up: If an LLM's context window is like a suitcase with limited space, what happens if you try to cram in way more than it can hold?

options.text: Everything fits perfectly no matter how much you add
You either can't fit it all, or you crowd out the important stuff with junk
The suitcase magically grows bigger for free

exercises.explain: Just like a suitcase, a context window has limited space. Overpacking it with unnecessary information means important items get squeezed out or left behind -- exactly the problem context compression solves.

cards.title: Every Word Costs Money
cards.body: Imagine paying a few cents every time you send a text message, and getting charged MORE for longer messages. That's basically how LLMs work with tokens. Sending bloated, unnecessary context quietly drains your budget every single call.

cards.title: What Is Context Compression?
cards.body: Context compression means shrinking the text you send to an LLM (fewer tokens) while keeping the important meaning intact. You drop filler, summarize, or trim so more useful information can fit, and each request costs less.

cards.title: Packing a Suitcase for a Trip
cards.body: An LLM's context window is like a suitcase with a strict size limit. If you cram in everything you own, you can't fit the outfit you actually need, and it costs extra baggage fees. Compression means folding smart: keep only what matters, ditch the bulk.

cards.title: Before and After
cards.body: Instead of pasting an entire 20-page manual into the prompt, you compress it to a 3-sentence summary of the relevant section. The model still answers correctly, but you used a fraction of the tokens and left room for the user's actual question and chat history.

cards.title: Myth: More Context Is Always Better
cards.body: Stuffing every possible fact into the prompt sounds safe, but it isn't. Extra irrelevant text raises cost, crowds out space for what truly matters, and increases 'context rot,' where the model gets confused or distracted by clutter and gives worse answers.

cards.title: Bloated vs Compressed Prompt
cards.body: Same question, two very different prompt sizes and outcomes.

left.text: 5,000 tokens of raw logs, high cost, model gets distracted, misses the real answer

right.text: 300 tokens of the key facts, low cost, model stays focused and answers accurately

exercises.prompt: Why do LLM engineers bother compressing context before sending it to the model?

options.text: To make the prompt look more professional
To fit more relevant info in, lower token cost, and reduce the chance of context rot
Because shorter prompts always give funnier answers
To make the model respond slower on purpose

exercises.explain: Compression exists for three main reasons: it frees up space for the information that actually matters, it reduces the token cost of each request, and it lowers the risk of context rot, where too much clutter confuses the model.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Compressing context can help reduce the risk of context rot by removing irrelevant clutter from the prompt.
exercises.explain: True. Context rot often happens when a model has to sift through too much irrelevant text. Compression removes that clutter, helping the model focus on what actually matters.

exercises.sentence: Reducing token count through compression helps cut cost and lowers exposure to context rot.
exercises.explain: Context rot describes how a model's performance degrades when its context window is cluttered with too much irrelevant or excessive information. Compression directly reduces this risk by keeping only what's needed.

exercises.prompt: Which of these summaries was most likely written by an AI trying to compress a long document, versus a rough human note?

options.text: "idk read the doc, its long, has stuff about pricing and returns i think"
"The document outlines the product's pricing tiers, refund policy, and shipping timelines in three clearly separated sections."
"pricing... returns... shipping... something else maybe idk skim it"

exercises.explain: The AI-style compression is clean, structured, and information-dense without extra filler words -- it captures the key topics precisely. The other two options are vague, informal human scribbles that don't actually compress meaning well.

exercises.prompt: A support-bot's context window is stuffed with 50 old chat transcripts before every new question. What's the FULL set of problems this causes?

options.text: Only higher dollar cost per request, nothing else
Higher token cost, less room for the actually relevant info, and more risk of context rot confusing the model
No real problems as long as the transcripts are text
Only slower typing speed for the user

exercises.explain: Stuffing the context window with irrelevant history triples the pain: you pay for tokens you don't need, you crowd out room for the relevant facts, and the flood of noise makes context rot more likely, causing the model to miss or misuse important details. Compression fixes all three by keeping the window lean and focused. That wraps up how to think about context compression's core motivation!
